acts.name: Indian Penal Code
acts.description: The IPC is the official criminal code of India. It is a comprehensive code intended to cover all substantive aspects of criminal law.
acts.simplified_explanation: Think of the IPC as the 'Rulebook of Wrongs'. it defines what acts are crimes (like theft, murder, or fraud) and tells you exactly what the punishment will be for each.

sections.title: Murder
sections.meaning: Culpable homicide is murder if the act by which the death is caused is done with the intention of causing death...
ingredients: Intention of causing such bodily injury as is likely to cause death
Knowledge that the act is imminently dangerous
sections.example: A shoots Z with the intention of killing him. A commits murder.
cases: K.M. Nanavati v. State of Maharashtra
State of AP v. R Punnayya

sections.title: Rape
sections.meaning: A man is said to commit 'rape' if he has sexual intercourse with a woman under certain circumstances...
ingredients: With her consent obtained by threat
sections.example: Intercourse obtained through threat of arrest.
cases: State of Punjab v. Gurmit Singh
Independent Thought v. Union of India

acts.name: Constitution of India
acts.description: The supreme law of India establishing principal political code, structure, procedures, and duties of government institutions.
acts.simplified_explanation: The Constitution is the 'Grandparent of all Laws'. Every other law in India must follow it. It gives citizens their basic rights and tells the government how to run the country.

sections.title: Right to Life
sections.meaning: No person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law.
sections.example: Right to shelter, right to privacy are extensions of Art 21.
cases: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
Justice K.S. Puttaswamy (Retd.) v. Union of India

acts.name: Indian Evidence Act
acts.description: Governs the admissibility of evidence in Indian Courts of Law.
acts.simplified_explanation: The Evidence Act is the 'Filter of Truth'. In court, you can't just say anything; this law decides which facts or documents the judge is allowed to look at to decide a case.

sections.title: Definition of Evidence
sections.meaning: 'Evidence' means and includes all statements which the Court permits or requires to be made before it by witnesses...
sections.example: A CCTV footage showing a theft is documentary (digital) evidence.
cases: State of NCT of Delhi v. Navjot Sandhu

acts.name: Code of Civil Procedure
acts.description: The law of procedure relating to the administration of civil proceedings in India.
acts.simplified_explanation: The CPC is the 'Roadmap for Civil Rights'. If someone owes you money or there's a property dispute, the CPC tells you exactly which papers to file and which court to go to.

sections.title: Courts to try all civil suits
sections.meaning: The Courts shall have jurisdiction to try all suits of a civil nature excepting suits of which their cognizance is either expressly or impliedly barred.
sections.example: A suit for declaration of title to land is a civil suit.
cases: P.M.A. Metropolitan v. Moran Mar Marthoma

acts.name: Code of Criminal Procedure
acts.description: Procedural law for providing a machinery for the investigation of crime, apprehension of suspected criminals, collection of evidence, etc.
acts.simplified_explanation: CrPC is the 'Police & Court Handbook'. While IPC defines the crime, CrPC tells the police how to arrest someone and tells the judge how to conduct a trial.

sections.title: Information in cognizable cases
sections.meaning: Every information relating to the commission of a cognizable offence, if given orally to an officer in charge of a police station, shall be reduced to writing...
sections.example: Reporting a robbery at the police station starts the FIR process under Sec 154.
cases: Lalita Kumari v. Govt. of U.P.
